stories.headline: Pony.ai launches PonyWorld 2.0 to advance autonomous driving technology
stories.summary: Chinese self-driving technology company Pony.ai has introduced PonyWorld 2.0, an upgraded version of its core training platform designed to improve autonomous driving capabilities. The new platform aims to enhance the company's machine learning and simulation systems for developing more robust self-driving vehicles.
stories.original_title: [Verdict] Pony.ai rolls out PonyWorld 2.0 to boost self-driving technology — Chinese self-driving tech firm Pony.ai has introduced PonyWorld 2.0, a new version of its core training platform for autonomous driving.
stories.context: Self-driving cars need to learn like humans do—by practicing over and over again—but it would take hundreds of years on real roads to teach them everything they need to know. To solve this, tech companies build huge digital training grounds where cars can practice millions of scenarios in computer simulations, from snow storms to children running into the street. Pony.ai is a Chinese company that's been racing against American giants like Google and Tesla to build these self-driving systems, and their original training platform (PonyWorld 1.0) has been the foundation for their progress so far. This upgrade to version 2.0 is essentially giving their computer system a bigger brain and better tools to react to the unpredictable nature of real-world driving.
stories.why_it_matters: This matters because better self-driving technology could make our roads much safer and transportation more efficient for everyone. Faster progress means robot cars could become a normal part of daily life sooner rather than later.
stories.other_side: Some people worry that rushing self-driving technology before it's fully tested could put people in danger and cost jobs for human drivers.

stories.headline: ESA's Proba-3 satellites generate 57 artificial solar eclipses, capture solar wind data
stories.summary: Since July 2025, the European Space Agency's Proba-3 satellite pair has successfully created 57 artificial solar eclipses while collecting over 250 hours of high-resolution solar atmosphere footage. The mission has produced surprising findings about the speed of solar wind, advancing our understanding of solar dynamics.
stories.original_title: [Esa.int] First Proba-3 science: surprisingly speedy solar wind — Since July 2025, the European Space Agency's pair of Proba-3 satellites has already created 57 artificial solar eclipses. So far, the mission has collected more than 250 hours of high-resolution videos of the Sun's atmo…
stories.context: The sun's outer atmosphere, called the corona, is normally invisible to us because the sun's surface is so bright—like trying to see stars while staring at a streetlamp. Scientists get rare natural opportunities to study this mysterious layer during solar eclipses when the moon blocks the sun's main light, but these only happen for a few minutes every couple of years in specific locations. To solve this problem, the European Space Agency launched twin satellites that fly in precise formation to create artificial eclipses whenever they want, essentially using one satellite to cast a shadow on the other. The "solar wind" they're studying is a stream of invisible particles streaming from the sun that causes beautiful aurora lights on Earth but can also disrupt our satellites and power grids if it gets too strong.
stories.why_it_matters: This matters because understanding the sun better helps us predict dangerous solar storms that could knock out power grids and satellites worldwide. The data collected will protect our technology and keep communications working.
stories.other_side: Critics argue that space missions are very expensive and the money could be used for problems here on Earth instead of studying the sun.

stories.headline: Samsung's Ocean mode feature explained: Does it make Galaxy phones waterproof?
stories.summary: Samsung has introduced Ocean Mode to select Galaxy phones through an Expert RAW update, sparking questions about whether the new feature enhances waterproof capabilities. Samsung Mobile has clarified that Ocean Mode is specifically designed for underwater photography and does not change a phone's water resistance rating.
stories.original_title: [SamMobile] Does Samsung's Ocean mode make your Galaxy phone waterproof? — You've probably seen the recent headlines about Ocean Mode coming to select Samsung Galaxy phones through an Expert RAW update. But even if it rings a bell, you might not know what it actually does or where it came from. Here's everything you need to know. Wh…
stories.context: Water-resistant phones have a rating system called IP (Ingress Protection) that tells you how much water a device can handle before it breaks—a bit like a waterproof watch rating. However, when you take photos underwater, light behaves differently, making colors look weird and pictures appear blue or greenish, which is why professional divers use special camera settings. Samsung's Expert RAW is a feature that lets photographers capture raw, unprocessed images for more editing control, and they added an "Ocean Mode" to automatically fix the color issues that happen when shooting underwater. Importantly, just because a phone has special camera settings for underwater photography doesn't mean the phone itself is suddenly protected from water damage—like having underwater sunglasses doesn't mean you can breathe underwater.
stories.why_it_matters: This is important because people might think their phones are suddenly waterproof and damage them by taking them deeper underwater than they should. It shows how companies need to be very clear about what new features actually do.
stories.other_side: Some customers feel misled that a feature called "Ocean Mode" doesn't actually make their phone more water-resistant than before.

stories.headline: ClawBench AI agent evaluation framework launched on PyPI
stories.summary: The ClawBench evaluation framework has been added to PyPI, designed to test whether AI agents can complete everyday online tasks effectively. The benchmark aims to provide standardized testing capabilities for measuring AI agent performance in real-world digital environments.
stories.original_title: [Pypi.org] clawbench-eval added to PyPI — ClawBench: Can AI Agents Complete Everyday Online Tasks? (alias of claw-bench)
stories.context: AI agents are computer programs designed to actually do things for you online—like booking a restaurant reservation or buying tickets—rather than just answering questions like a typical chatbot. The problem has been that it's hard to tell which AI agents are actually good at real tasks versus which ones just sound impressive in conversation. PyPI (Python Package Index) is essentially a giant warehouse of free software tools that programmers can download to help build their own applications, making it easy to share useful code. ClawBench is like creating a standardized test for these AI agents—similar to how schools use the SAT or ACT—to measure whether these digital assistants can successfully complete everyday tasks that real humans do on websites and apps.
stories.why_it_matters: This matters because we need good ways to test if AI helpers can actually do useful tasks for us in real life. Better testing helps companies build AI that's more helpful and less likely to mess things up.
stories.other_side: Some experts worry that standardized tests might make AI too focused on passing exams rather than being truly useful in unique situations.

stories.headline: Harness benchmark for AI agent task completion released on PyPI
stories.summary: The Harness benchmark framework has been released on PyPI, building upon the ClawBench initiative to evaluate AI agents' capabilities in completing online tasks. This alias package provides developers with additional tools for testing and benchmarking AI agent performance across various digital scenarios.
stories.original_title: [Pypi.org] harness-bench added to PyPI — ClawBench: Can AI Agents Complete Everyday Online Tasks? (alias of claw-bench)
stories.context: In the software world, when programmers want to use a tool, they give it a specific name to find it in the code libraries—like how a book has a title you search for in a library catalog. Sometimes, a project gets renamed or needs an alternative name for different reasons, like when a product gets rebranded or when the original name wasn't clear enough about what the tool does. This "Harness" framework is essentially the same evaluation system as ClawBench but released under a different name—like how a movie might have different titles in different countries. The fact that both are now available on PyPI means more developers will find and use them, which helps everyone agree on how to measure whether AI agents are actually useful or just overhyped.
stories.why_it_matters: More testing tools mean developers can build AI that actually works well for everyday tasks instead of just sounding smart in conversations. This pushes AI technology forward in practical ways that help real people.
stories.other_side: Critics say that too many different testing standards might confuse developers and make it harder to compare AI systems against each other.

stories.headline: North Korean APT37 hacking group uses Facebook social engineering to distribute RokRAT malware
stories.summary: Security researchers have tracked a new multi-stage campaign by North Korean hacking group APT37 that leverages Facebook social engineering tactics to infect targets with RokRAT malware. Threat actors establish friendships on the social media platform to deliver malicious payloads through sophisticated manipulation techniques.
stories.original_title: [Internet] North Korea's APT37 Uses Facebook Social Engineering to Deliver RokRAT Malware — The North Korean hacking group tracked as APT37 (aka ScarCruft) has been attributed to a fresh multi-stage, social engineering campaign in which threat actors approached targets on Facebook and added them as friends on the social media platform, turning the t…
stories.context: North Korea, facing severe international economic sanctions, has turned to computer hacking as a way to steal money and gather intelligence for their government. "APT37" is how cybersecurity experts refer to one specific group of state-sponsored hackers from North Korea (APT stands for Advanced Persistent Threat, which is technical jargon for "a really skilled team that keeps trying to break in"). Social engineering is the fancy term for manipulating people into doing something they shouldn't—like how a con artist might pretend to be your friend to learn your secrets. This campaign shows how these hackers are now using Facebook's friend system to build trust with targets before sending them a file that looks harmless but secretly installs "RokRAT"—a type of malicious software that lets the hackers remotely control and spy on the victim's computer.
stories.why_it_matters: This matters because hackers hiding behind fake Facebook friendships can steal sensitive information from regular people who let their guard down. Everyone needs to be careful about who they trust online, even on friendly social platforms.
stories.other_side: Some privacy advocates worry that stories like this could scare people away from social media or make them too suspicious of genuine online connections.

stories.headline: Oppo introduces first smartphone with touchscreen functional in pouring rain
stories.summary: Oppo has achieved a significant breakthrough in smartphone innovation by releasing the world's first phone with a touchscreen that remains fully functional during heavy rainfall. The technology addresses a common daily problem experienced by smartphone users worldwide.
stories.original_title: [Notebookcheck.net] First phone whose touchscreen can be used in pouring rain comes from Oppo — Oppo has been at the forefront of phone innovation lately and keeps notching 'firsts' in its device portfolio. It has now managed to solve a pesky daily problem that every phone user has encountered.
stories.context: Every smartphone owner knows the frustration of trying to use a wet touchscreen in the rain—the screen either doesn't respond at all or starts going rogue because water droplets confuse the phone into thinking you're touching it everywhere. Touchscreens work by detecting the tiny electrical charge in your fingertips, but water also conducts electricity, which tricks the phone into thinking the entire surface is being touched. Manufacturers have tried various solutions like special coatings and settings, but these typically only help with light splashes or require you to turn off parts of the screen. What Oppo has accomplished is creating new screen technology that can tell the difference between touch and water, similar to how your brain can tell the difference between someone tapping your shoulder vs. rain falling on it—even though both involve physical contact.
stories.why_it_matters: This matters because getting stuck with a phone that won't work in the rain is a frustrating problem that happens to millions of people. This technology makes phones more reliable in everyday bad weather situations.
stories.other_side: Some people question if this special screen is really necessary or just another feature that raises the price of smartphones without solving a major problem.

stories.headline: Lumentum receives aggressive target revision from tech analysts
stories.summary: Lumentum Corporation has experienced one of the most aggressive target price revisions in the technology sector, reflecting significant changes in market expectations. The optical component company's revised target highlights shifting investor sentiment toward its business prospects.
stories.original_title: [Yahoo Entertainment] Why Lumentum (LITE) Just Saw One of the Most Aggressive Target Revisions in Tech —
stories.context: Wall Street analysts are professional researchers whose job is to predict how much a company's stock will be worth in the future, kind of like how weather forecasters predict the weather. They give their "target price"—usually a range of about 12 months into the future—which tells investors what they think a fair price would be for buying or selling shares. Analysts usually adjust these predictions gradually based on news about the company, like earning more money or launching a new product. An "aggressive" revision means the analysts suddenly changed their prediction by a huge amount—like jumping from predicting partly cloudy skies to predicting a hurricane—suggesting something major happened to either make the company look much more valuable or much less valuable than everyone previously thought.
stories.why_it_matters: This matters because when analysts dramatically change their predictions about a company, it can cause huge swings in the stock price and affect regular investors' savings. It shows how uncertain and fast-changing the tech market can be.
stories.other_side: Some investors think analyst predictions are often wrong and shouldn't be trusted, since they're just guessing about the future.

stories.headline: Press organizations urge withdrawal of IT Rules amendment over free speech concerns
stories.summary: Multiple press bodies have demanded the unconditional withdrawal of proposed amendments to the IT Rules, citing threats to freedom of expression and pointing to recent takedown orders as evidence of potential misuse. The organizations are specifically calling for closure of the Sahyog Portal used for content regulation compliance.
stories.original_title: [MediaNama.com] Press bodies demand 'unconditional withdrawal' of draft amendment to IT Rules, warns of free speech threat — Highlighting the recent takedown orders, the press bodies warned how the draft amendments to the IT Rules threatens free speech and demanded their withdrawal along with the closure of the Sahyog Portal.
stories.context: Governments everywhere are struggling with how to regulate internet content—social media posts, news articles, videos, and comments—because they want to control harmful content without crushing free speech. IT Rules are like laws that explain what online platforms are legally required to do when governments ask them to remove or block certain content that officials deem problematic. The "Sahyog Portal" mentioned here is an official government website where these content removal orders are tracked and processed—like a digital reception desk for government requests. Press organizations (groups of news outlets and journalists) are speaking out because they're worried that new changes to these rules could make it easier for governments to silently suppress news stories and opinions they don't like, similar to how a library might be told which books it's allowed to keep on its shelves.
stories.why_it_matters: This matters because new rules that limit what can be said online could affect everyone's ability to share information and opinions freely. It could change how news and speech work on the internet for years to come.
stories.other_side: Government officials argue that some rules are needed to stop the spread of harmful content and protect people from dangerous information online.
